coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional natural derived carbon quantum dots from Withania somnifera (L.) – Antiviral activities against SARS-CoV-2 pseudoviron
coredata.dc:creator: Nair, Akhila

coredata.dc:description: Natural carbon dots (NCQDs) are expediently significant in the photo-, nano- and biomedical spheres owing to their facile synthesis, optical and physicochemical attributes. In the present study, three NCQDs are prepared and optimized from Withania somnifera (ASH) by one-step hydrothermal (bottom-up) method: HASHP (without dopant), nitrogen doped HASHNH3 (surface passivation using ammonia) and HASHEDA (surface passivation with ethylenediamine). The HR-TEM images reveal that HASHP, HASNH3, HASHEDA are spherically shaped with 2.5 ± 0.5 nm, 4 ± 1 nm and 5 ± 2 nm particle size, respectively, whereas FTIR confirms the aqueous solubility and nitrogen doping. The XRD patterns ensure that the NCQDs are amorphous and graphitic in nature. Comparatively, HASHNH3 (32.5%) and HASHEDA (27.6%) portray better fluorescence quantum yield than HASHP (5.6%). The increase in quantum yield for the doped NCQDs can be attributed to the surface passivation using ammonia and ethylenediamine. Surface passivation plays a crucial role in enhancing the fluorescence properties of quantum dots. The introduction of nitrogen through ammonia and ethylenediamine provides additional electronic states, possibly reducing non-radiative recombination sites and hence boosting the QY. In addition, an antiviral study unveils the striking potential of surface passivated NCQDs to curb Covid-19 crises with around 85% inhibition of SARS-CoV pseudoviron cells, which is better in comparison to the non-doped NCQDs. Hence, to understand the paramount efficacy of these NCQDs, a hypothesis on their possible mechanism of action against Covid-19 is discussed.

row.entry: A decisive contrivance - arrests the proliferation of respiratory syndrome virus as well as porcine reproductive virus

Suppresses the ROS production stimulated by pro-inflammatory cytokines and interferon stimulating genes (ISGs); inhibits the entry of porcine epidemic diarrhea virus (PED) by electrostatic interaction with virus along with the inhibition of the synthesis of negative strand viral RNA

Abrogate Covid-19 related cytokine storm and inflammation via reported mechanism including the modulation of NF-Kb, ROS scavenging, MAP Kinase, activation of protein expression nuclear factor erythroid 2 related factor 2(Nrf2)

Curcumin binds to carbon dots at ΔG = -7.6 kcal/mol but modification improved the binding affinity and %interaction (analyzed through molecular docking). These NCQDs increased the protection against SAR-CoV-2 Dimer based screening system and in vitro at MOI = 0.1

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural carbon dots (NCQDs) are expediently significant in the photo-, nano- and biomedical spheres owing to their facile synthesis, optical and physicochemical attributes. In the present study, three NCQDs are prepared and optimized from Withania somnifera (ASH) by one-step hydrothermal (bottom-up) method: HASHP (without dopant), nitrogen doped HASHNH3 (surface passivation using ammonia) and HASHEDA (surface passivation with ethylenediamine). The HR-TEM images reveal that HASHP, HASNH3, HASHEDA are spherically shaped with 2.5 ± 0.5 nm, 4 ± 1 nm and 5 ± 2 nm particle size, respectively, whereas FTIR confirms the aqueous solubility and nitrogen doping. The XRD patterns ensure that the NCQDs are amorphous and graphitic in nature. Comparatively, HASHNH3 (32.5%) and HASHEDA (27.6%) portray better fluorescence quantum yield than HASHP (5.6%). The increase in quantum yield for the doped NCQDs can be attributed to the surface passivation using ammonia and ethylenediamine. Surface passivation plays a crucial role in enhancing the fluorescence properties of quantum dots. The introduction of nitrogen through ammonia and ethylenediamine provides additional electronic states, possibly reducing non-radiative recombination sites and hence boosting the QY. In addition, an antiviral study unveils the striking potential of surface passivated NCQDs to curb Covid-19 crises with around 85% inhibition of SARS-CoV pseudoviron cells, which is better in comparison to the non-doped NCQDs. Hence, to understand the paramount efficacy of these NCQDs, a hypothesis on their possible mechanism of action against Covid-19 is discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has hitherto been able to display a nonavoidable plethora of potential that has not only outranked the conventional technologies but also invaded the scientific methodologies to bring a quantum leap in various applications. Annexes to these nanoscale level materials are carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have legion health as well as environmental benefits (Pal et al., 2018). Their counterparts like cadmium selenide (CdSe) have their own slew of problems including challenges in cell cytotoxicity, fluorescence blinking (due to surface defects), biodegradation, and tedious synthesis process (Amjad et al., 2019). Unlike their counterparts, CQDs have attained an enviable position due to the preeminent characteristics especially the imperial photostability, the entrancing fluorescence, the aqueous solubility, low photobleaching, the chemical stability, low toxicity, economical and minimalist synthesis process (Dager et al., 2020). Generally, the syntheses of CQDs are catalogued into two approaches: top-down and bottom-up. On the one hand, top-down approach defines the process in which the large carbon molecules are subjected to disintegration into smaller particles enrolling chemical oxidation, laser ablation, arc discharge. On the flip side, bottom-up approach involves synthesis through molecular carbon precursors, notably, thermal decomposition, microwave and hydrothermal (Ali et al., 2023; Dager et al., 2019).

In particular, the hydrothermal mode of synthesis, which is the most preferable, involving mild reactions, avoiding multiple steps, flexibility in incorporating green resources as well as developing CQDs with repeatability, good quantum yield, controllability in size, purity, composition, energy and very economical (Pal et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2020). In addition, water is utilized as the prime reaction medium to produce quasi-spherical nanoparticles within a size range of 10 nm under simple experimental conditions. Various reports suggest that the optical properties of natural carbon dots (NCQDs) are dependent on the chemical structure like surface functionalization by carboxyl, hydroxyl or nitrogen doping, size, pH and solvents and the natural carbon source. Natural carbon source contain non-carbon atoms that have replaced a carbon atom in the molecular frame of the molecules, usually, hetro atoms, which are responsible for different biological activities reflecting the natural carbon source selected (Wu et al., 2021). Surface functionalization, especially with heteroatom improves the optical properties, quantum yield, water solubility and stability. It is reported that nitrogen doped CQDs using ethylenediamine and Allium sativum increase stability over wide pH range (Dsouza et al., 2021). Similarly, Hylocereus undatus extract as carbon source and aqueous ammonia as nitrogen source prove the biological and catalytic activities (Arul et al., 2017). Besides, the natural carbon source can efficiently be attained from a broad range of raw materials (cheap and sustainable) in bulk quantities by hydrothermal synthesis (Prasannan and Imae, 2013). Natural carbon source including, Bombyx mori silk (Ren et al., 2018), Orange juice (Li et al., 2017a,b), Curcuma Longa L (Lu et al., 2021), Barley (Xie et al., 2019), Phyllanthus emblica (Arul and Sethuraman, 2019), Magnolia liliiflora (Atchudan et al., 2018), Acerola Fruit (Carvalho et al., 2019), Spinach (Ren et al., 2018), Hongcaitai (Li et al., 2018) Lemon juice (He et al., 2018), Kiwi Fruit Peel (Atchudan et al., 2021), Cocconut petiole residues (Gao et al., 2021), Cranberry beans (Zulfajri et al., 2019), Zucchini (Hoang and Gomes, 2019), Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract (Venkatesan et al., 2019), etc.

In this respect, Withania somnifera (L) Dunal. can be considered as a promising natural resource for obtaining CQDs. This plant is known as Indian winter cherry or Indian ginseng (Ahmad and Dar, 2017). This evergreen, woody shrub that attains a height of about 0.5–2 m belongs to the family of Solanaceae. It is mainly cultivated in subtropical and tropical zones including China, India, China, Srilanka, Middle east, Canary Island (Mediterranean), Africa, Europe and Australia (Dar and MuzamilAhmad, 2020). The Ayurvedic history of India reveals that the shrub has numerous health benefits due to their therapeutic activities. Evidence based studies also add strength to the case unveiling their efficacy especially roots in neurodegenerative diseases (stress, adaptogen, memory enhancer, insomnia, anxiety, schizophrenia, obsessive compulsive disorder, cognitive deficits), rheumatoid arthritis, infertility, growth in children, gout, subclinical hypothyroidism, diabetes, chemotherapy adjuvant etc (Huang et al., 2019). Usually, during the production of Withania somniferra water extract, the extract which did not attain the required specification like color, texture, purity for further processing or supply are discarded. Therefore, conversion of such waste into value added products is prudent (Tandon and Yadav, 2020).

Moreover, the antiviral activities of many NCQDs have researched among them, Xue et al. have discussed on the promising and upcoming CQDs. Their broad spectrum activities and the mechanism of action against various pathogenic virus especially coronavirus disease (Covid, 2019) (Yuxiang et al., 2022). Curcumin, is one the extensively studied NCQDs for antiviral activities. NCQDs prepared from curcumin through mild pyrolysis-induced polymerization and carbonization not only increased curcumin water solubility but also lowers the cytotoxicity. Besides, the antiviral properties of curcumin NCQDs were increased against Japanese encephalitis virus as studied by molecular docking studies (Chen et al., 2022).

With this frame of reference, an eco-friendly, economical CQDs of Withania somnifera (Ashwagandha) water extract (ASH-CQDs) were prepared by a facile, single step hydrothermal method. Besides, a comparative characterization data was constructed among plain ASH-CQDs, ammonia (NH3) doped CQDs and ethylenediamine (EDA) doped CQDs, HASHP, HASHNH3, HASHEDA respectively (Table 1 ). The characterization involved spectral properties, High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy. Further, the antiviral potential of the aforementioned formulations was evaluated on SARS-CoV-2 pseudoviron along with the plausible mechanism of action.

Despite the existence of numerous NCQDs derived from natural products, this study stands out in several ways. It is the first to prepare CDs using Withania somnifera, renowned for its diverse biological activities. Unique surface passivation methods, employing ammonia and ethylenediamine, are utilized and compared to comprehend their profound effects. The extraction of Withania somnifera, performed without harmful solvents, adds to the study's innovative approach. Most notably, the NCQDs demonstrate significant antiviral activities, particularly against SARS-CoV-2 Pseudoviron, offering a promising avenue for addressing critical pathogenic diseases like COVID-19. The research also highlights the potential for using industrial biowaste or byproducts in simple, cost-effective procedures, contributing to a sustainable and circular economy. This multifaceted novelty not only enhances the scientific community's understanding but also paves the way for impactful, real-world applications in battling life-threatening diseases and bolstering economic stability (Aswathi et al., 2023).

Ashwagandha water extract (ASH) was generously supplied by Plant Lipids Private Limited, Cochin, India, while HPLC-grade Ammonia (NH3) and Ethylenediamine (EDA), along with various other chemicals, were acquired from Merck, India. Throughout the entire study, Millipore-MilliQ distilled water was used.
A green, facile and one-pot hydrothermal method (Bottom-up approach) was adopted the preparation of CQDs. Basically, three formulations were prepared:

Formulation I - Preparation of HASHP: 5 g of ASH was diluted with 300 ml of Milli pore-Milli Q water under high shear for about 10 min at 30 °C using PRIMIX-Homomixer mark 11.2.5 homogenizer (SPX flow technology Rosista GmbH, Zechenstrasse 49, DE 5942 Unna, Germany) in Teflon coated autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h.

Formulation II - Preparation of HASHNH
3
: 5 g of ASH was diluted with 300 ml of Milli pore-MilliQ water under high shear for 10 min at 30 °C 0.1 ml of NH3 solution was added to the suspension, stirred well and autoclaved for 180 °C for 12 h.

Formulation III - Preparation of HASHEDA: 5 g of ASH was dissolved in 300 ml of Millipore-MilliQ water under high shear for 10 min at 30 °C.1 ml of EDA was added and stirred well followed by hydrothermal treatment in the autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C.

After the completion of the process, the resultant black carbonised solution of all the three formulations, were allowed to cool down to room temperature followed by filtration through 0.1 μm syringe filter to remove larger particles. The filtrates thus obtained were brownish yellow in color, and were further dialysed in 500 ml Millipore-MilliQ water using a regenerated seamless cellulose dialysis membrane with molecular cutoff 12000 to 14000 and pore size 2.4 nm for 6hrs with hourly replacement of the dialysis water. The CQDs, thus obtained were stored at 4 °C for further studies (Niu et al., 2015; Vasimalai et al., 2018).

The UV–visible and fluorescence characteristics were assessed using two different instruments: a JASCO V-550 UV–visible spectrophotometer and a JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorometer. The fluorescence spectrofluorometer was equipped with a xenon discharge lamp and a 1 cm quartz cell, operated at room temperature around 298 K. To maintain consistency, the excitation and emission slit widths for all fluorescence measurements were set to 5 nm (He et al., 2018).

The fluorescent quantum yield of HASHP, HASHNH3 and HASHEDA were calculated by using the comparative method of William et al. (Williams et al., 1983). In this connection quantum yield was calculated as mentioned by equation:
QYS = (FSARQYR) / (FRAS)
In this context, the FS represents the fluorescence emission integrated over the sample, while the FR corresponds to the integrated fluorescence emission of the reference substance, which is Quinine sulphate. AS indicates the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the sample, and AR represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the reference substance. Furthermore, QS denotes the quantum yield of the sample, whereas QR signifies the quantum yield of the reference fluorophore. It's worth noting that Quinine sulphate was selected as the reference standard with a known quantum yield of 54%.

High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) of make Jeol/JEM 2100, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan was utilized to study the morphology to the synthesized CQDs. A uniformly distributed droplet from each sample was taken and dried on the glow discharged HR-TEM microgrid having thin carbon coating at room temperature. Subsequently, the morphology of each sample was observed (Vasimalai et al., 2018).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance instrument located in Karlsruhe, Germany. The samples were dried at 60 °C before analysis, and Cu Kα radiation with λ = 0.1542 was used at room temperature. The scanning speed was set at 1.2°/min, covering a 2θ range from 3° to 70° (Sun et al., 2020). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra for HASHP, HASHNH3, and HASHEDA were conducted using a JASCO FT/IR-460 plus apparatus located in Mumbai, India. The spectral recordings were performed in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with 32 scans for each sample. Prior to analysis, an appropriate amount of KBr was dried under an IR lamp and mixed with each sample in a 4:1 ratio. For Raman spectroscopy measurements, CQDs (Carbon Quantum Dots) were prepared on a glass substrate and then analyzed using STR 500 nm focal length laser Raman spectroscopy (Arul and Sethuraman, 2018).

The samples were prepared by diluting them at a ratio of 1:1000. Subsequently, these diluted samples were mixed with DMEM supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum to assess their cytotoxicity in DLD 1 cells. The DLD 1 cells, expressing human ACE2, were cultivated on 96 glass bottom plates and allowed to grow for 24 h. Afterward, the cells were treated with Hoechst 33342, a fluorescent nuclear dye, at a concentration of 5 μg/ml for 5 min. Following a wash, fresh medium was added, and the cells were kept in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 4 h. The test samples were introduced to the cells at the specified concentration. Fluorescent images were taken at 48 h using an inverted Fluorescent Microscope, Nikon TiE, and a DAPI filter set to visualize cell death. An EMCCD camera from Andor, along with NIS element software (Nikon), was used to capture the images. The evaluation of cell death was based on the degree of condensed chromatin compared to the control wells that were left untreated (Sobhan et al., 2013).

Unlike other luminescent materials including silver nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles, etc., NCQDs follow an interesting excitation dependent photoluminescence which depend upon surface defect emission and zigzag site emission. The fluorescence spectra exhibit the maximum emission maximum (λem) at 320 nm for HASHP, 380 nm for HASHNH3 and 380 nm for HASHEDA. They are stable in aqueous solution for about two years without any change in their optical properties. The emission profile of HASHP, HASHNH3, and HASHEDA is depicted in Fig. 2 which denotes different excitation wavelengths (He et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017a,b).

The measured fluorescence quantum yields of HASHP, HASHNH3, and HASHEDA dots are 35.6, 32.4, and 27.6, respectively. In addition, as Prado et al. (2023), illustrated that doping heteroatomic impurities into the carbon core of the NCQDs result in change in their electronic structure, which changes the surface to give increased fluorescence. Ammonia doping, offers five valence electrons to alter the carbon core. This results in the formation of amine functional group on to the surface that contributes towards enhanced optical properties. The quantum yield, therefore, of HASHNH3 have higher quantum yield than HASHEDA. Another analogy between HASHP, HASHNH3 and HASHEDA, suggests that doping is not so necessary for ASH-NCQDs. Hence, the reactions to prepare ASH-NCQDs are easy, economic, environment friendly and cost effective (Williams et al., 1983).

The HR-TEM images displayed spherical CQDs with a uniform shape and no noticeable aggregation (Fig. 3 ). The particle sizes of HASHP, HASHN3, and HASHEDA were measured to be 2.5 ± 0.5 nm, 4 ± 1 nm and 5 ± 2 nm respectively. HR-TEM analysis revealed a consistent lattice d-spacing of 0.19 nm for all the CQDs, providing evidence that the obtained NCQDs form a successful sp2 conjugated structure from unconjugated ASH. This conclusion is further supported by the close agreement of these results with the graphite lattice plane (100) (Gedda et al., 2023).

XRD patterns of the three synthesized C-dots have been also studied, and the obtained results are given in Fig. 4 . The broad reflection peaks of HASHP, HASHNH3, HASHEDA are located at 25° which is analogues to the graphitic carbon (002) plane which is because of the small size and amorphous nature of NCQDs. However, the reflection peaks of HASHNH3 and HASEDA are more prominent when compared to HASHP. Hence, the XRD patterns are well in agreement with the particle size obtained from HR-TEM. It also indicates that the numerous functional groups present support the immense stable nature of these NCQDs without visible aggregation (Shukla et al., 2020; Shellaiah et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2020).

The FT-IR analysis of hydrothermally synthesized NCQDs confirms the presence of diverse functional groups, which can be attributed to the influence of the starting material or included precursors. Fig. 5 illustrates the FT-IR spectra of various samples. In the FT-IR spectrum of ASH, prominent absorption bands were observed at 3309 cm−1, corresponding to O–H vibrational stretching, as well as C–H bending peaks at 2903 cm−1 and 2128 cm−1. Vibrational stretching peaks of CO were detected at 1993 cm−1 to 1946 cm−1, along with CC/C–O peaks at 1616 cm−1 to 1638 cm−1. Additionally, C–N bending vibrations were evident at 1418 cm−1, and C–O bending vibrations were observed at 1031 cm−1. For HASHEDA, the FT-IR spectrum showed distinctive shifts compared to ASH. Notably, the O–H and N–H vibrational peaks were found at 3338 cm−1, indicating a shift from the 3309 cm−1 peak observed in ASH. This shift can be attributed to the conversion of phenolic –OH to ammonium phenolate, suggesting the presence of more nitrogen molecules or dopants in HASHEDA. Moreover, the C–H bending peaks also shifted from 2903 cm−1 to 2297 cm−1 in HASHEDA. The FTIR analysis revealed several characteristic absorption bands in the spectrum. At 1738 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1, there were broad absorption bands indicating CO sp2 vibrational stretching. The symmetric stretching vibration of –COOH was observed at 1412 cm−1, while the C–OH group showed an absorption peak at 1216 cm−1. For the FTIR spectrum of HASHNH3, two broad peaks were identified at 3339 cm−1, suggesting O–H and N–H vibrational stretching. These peaks indicated the presence of numerous amine and hydroxyl groups. Additionally, at 2302 cm−1, C–H bending peaks were observed. Moreover, a shifted and broadened CO/C–C vibrational stretching was noted at 1610 cm−1, which may be attributed to the carbonyl group's involvement in the formation of NCQDs (N-doped carbon quantum dots). Symmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH were found at 1408 cm−1, and a sharp peak was noticed at 1222 cm−1, indicating the presence of C–OH groups. The FTIR spectrum of HASHP exhibited distinct vibrational features. The O–H stretching peak was observed to shift from 3309 cm−1 to 3341 cm−1. This shift could possibly be attributed to the formation of NCQDs. Additionally, C–H bending peaks appeared at 2301 cm−1, and vibrational stretching peaks of CC/C–O were observed at 1636 cm−1. Symmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH were detected at 1450 cm−1, and C–OH vibrations were evident at 1222 cm−1. Comparing the functional intensities of ASH with HASHP, HASHNH3, and HASHEDA, it was observed that the intensities of ASH were reduced, broadened, and shifted in comparison to the prepared NCQDs. This effect can be attributed to the presence of phenol (-OH), carbonyl groups, and Ammonium phenolate (-N-H) (in the case of a dopant used) in combination with the components of the starting material that remained intact. (Hoang and Gomes, 2019; Shukla et al., 2020).

The Raman spectra of NCQDs were analyzed using a Gaussian function, as shown in Fig. 6 . HASHP CQDs exhibited a D band at 1350 cm−1 and a G band at 1567 cm−1. Similarly, HASHNH3 showed a D band at 1348 cm−1 and a G band at 1577 cm−1, while HASHEDA displayed a D band at 1348 cm−1 and a G band at 1550 cm−1. These bands correspond to sp3 and sp2 carbon, respectively. The D band represents A1g symmetry photons near the K-zone boundary, while the G band corresponds to the E2g vibrational mode of sp2 carbon. HASHP, with the lowest ID/IG ratio (0.77), shows relatively more structural order, while HASHEDA, with the highest ratio (0.89), exhibits more disorder. HASHNH3 falls in between with a 0.85 ratio, indicating moderate structural disorder. These assessments, based on the principle linking higher ID/IG ratios to increased material disorder, require additional analysis and consideration of other factors for a comprehensive understanding, as Raman spectroscopy solely provides insight into structural disorder. These results suggest the presence of vacant lattice sites of sp3 carbon (Arul and Sethuraman, 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism of action to combat SARS-CoV-2 (Covid-19) pandemic
ce:section.ce:para: In December 2019, an outbreak of a highly contagious virus called Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) occurred in Wuhan, China. This virus is responsible for causing Coronavirus Disease 2019, commonly known as Covid-19 (Atchudan et al., 2016). According to data from the World Health Organization (WHO), the global impact of the disease has been extensive, affecting approximately 276 million people, with a tragic death toll of about 5 million individuals. As the pandemic progressed, the virus underwent mutations, resulting in seven variants known as Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, Lambda, Mu, and Omicron. These variants have not only affected humans but also had implications for animals. (World Health Organization) The term ‘corona’ meaning ‘crown like’ comes from its morphology as this virus has protruding transmembrane spike glycoproteins (S-proteins) (Wadhwa et al., 2020). Covid-19, is termed as the global health emergency as it intruded all spheres of normal human life and thrashed the economy (Atchudan et al., 2016). This international pandemic that infected millions and escalated death toll evoked an urgent antidote in either the form of novel drugs, vaccines or reutilizing of the prevailing drugs (Wadhwa et al., 2020). Amidst this, an alternative and favourable podium with the support of herbal or medicinal plants was essential to limit the lethal effects of Covid-19 (Dhawan et al., 2021).

Withania somnifera, contains a variety of biologically active compounds known as secondary metabolites, with notable ones being withanone, withanolide A, withanolide D, and withaferin A. These compounds have demonstrated a wide range of biological activities, including anti-inflammatory, anti-tumorigenic, pro-apoptotic, anti-invasive, anti-angiogenic, anti-microbial, anti-diabetic, hepatoprotective, and anti-viral properties. Notably, in a study conducted by Kambizi et al., in 2007, the antiviral activity of Withania somnifera was reported against Herpes Simplex Virus Type-1. This highlights the potential of this herb in combating viral infections (Paul et al., 2021). Whilst, NCQDs are employed due to evidences that are favourable in ameliorating the antiviral properties of ASH in terms of detectability under a particular wavelength of light; flexible size ranging from 1 to 10 nm, tuneable shapes which help penetrate or target SARS-CoV-2. In addition, the cationic charges of CQDs/NCQDs can be advantageous in two respect, primarily to disable or sequester the S-protein of the virus and secondarily, to conveniently associate with the negative RNA strand of the virus, resulting in the manufacturing of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Manivannan and Ponnuchamy, 2020; Nangan et al., 2023). In this view, it is vital to understand the prime targets responsible for the entry and replication of coronavirus and subsequently, the mode of action of NCQDS to deal with the contagious coronavirus.

The unit S1 of the glycoprotein cleaves to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2), a cellular host receptor and/or the priming of S-protein by transmembrane protease serine 2 (TMPRSS2), a host cellular protease at the S2 and S1/S2 site and this marks the initiation and propagation of infection. Another possible yet insignificant cause of entrance and thereby spread of SARS-CoV-2 is by the priming of glycoprotein by endothelial proteases CatB/L. However, the major focus of current studies is to target and suppress TMPRSS2 as its growth not only promote human subtypes but also encourages avian influenza virus. Besides, as the virus enters the host cell, the two overlapping polyproteins known as pp1a and pp1ab releases the viral genome for replication and transcription through immense proteolytic activity that is channelled by main protease (Mpro) of SARS-CoV-2. Therefore, Mpro, is another most attractive target mainly at their preserved substrate binding site to restrict viral replication. For different coronaviruses there are various identical residues including Asp187, Glu166, Gln192, His41, His172, Tyr54 at their substrate binding pockets. They are almost structurally similar in human as well as animal coronaviruses in context of functional tertiary structure and primary amino acid sequence (Manivannan and Ponnuchamy, 2020).

In the spotlight of current attention, recent findings propose that CQDs (Carbon Quantum Dots) or NCQDs (Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots) demonstrate advantageous effects against porcine reproductive virus, pseudorabies virus, and respiratory syndrome virus, highlighting their potential as antiviral agents (Table 3 ). Moreover, research indicates that CQDs can inhibit viral replication by stimulating the activation of interferon-stimulated genes, leading to an increased production of interferon-α (Kotta et al., 2020). The necessary quantity of functional groups present on the surface of CQDs/NCDs interacts with the S-protein of human coronavirus-229E/SARS-CoV-2. This interaction serves to hinder the entry and genomic replication of viral RNA, while also enabling viral biosensing due to the unique fluorescent properties of these groups (Dhanjal et al., 2021). Moreover, the potent bioactive constituents present in ASH, namely wihaferin-A and withanone-N, show significant promise in trilateral binding with TRMRSS2. This trilateral binding mechanism involves three crucial actions:(i) Interacting with TMPRSS2, they effectively obstruct the virus's entry into the host cell. (ii) They downregulate TMPRSS2 expression, further impeding the virus's ability to infect the host. (iii) These bioactive components also reduce the survival opportunities of the virus by inhibiting the Mpro protein (Saggam et al., 2021).

Notably, withanone-N exhibits remarkable efficiency in directly binding with the SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro) with a binding energy comparable to that of the known Mpro inhibitor (N3). Analogously, there are various other anti-inflammatory, antiviral, anti-tumorigenic mechanism reported for withaferin-A and with none–N including inactivation of NF-Kb, activation of p53 and pRB (tumour suppressor proteins), oxidative stress, DNA damage repair, decreasing cytokine secretion in lungs, unchanging the ACE2 expression in lungs which may be strong factors in retarding the SARS-CoV-2 virus (Balkrishna et al., 2021). and alleviating the catastrophic effects of Covid-19 (Straughn and Kakar, 2020). Accordingly, the NCQDs of ASH could be promising in strongly impeding the entry and replication of Coronavirus-19. In the current study, the synergistic effect of ASH (either nitrogen doped or undoped) with NCQDs have demonstrated inhibition from 75% to 85% of SARS-CoV-2 pseudoviron cells. Hence, these suggested mechanisms could potentially provide a rationale for the effectiveness of ASH-NCQDs in addressing Covid-19 (Fig. 8 ). Additionally, it is advisable to conduct clinical assessments to evaluate its efficiency in treating Covid-19 patients (Saber-Moghaddam et al., 2021).

This study used a green, cost-effective hydrothermal method to create NCQs from Withania somnifera, employing ultra-pure water with/without NH3 and EDA as passivation agents. Optimal conditions were 180 °C for 12 h. The NCQ samples, ASH, HASHP, HASHNH3, and HASHEDA, all showcased vibrant blue under UV–vis light and consistent fluorescence emission, with quantum yields of 35.6, 32.4, and 27.6 respectively. HR-TEM images revealed their spherical, dispersed nature with sizes 2.5 ± 0.5 nm, 4 ± 1 nm, and 5 ± 2 nm for HASHP, HASHNH3, and HASHEDA respectively. XRD and FTIR results confirmed their small, amorphous nature and presence of functional groups, enhancing aqueous solubility. D and G bands indicated that HASHP and HASHNH3 are superior NCQDs compared to HASHEDA. Preliminary antiviral studies showed ASH-NCDs of HASHP and HASNNH3 strongly inhibiting virus entry and replication, marking them as potential resources for Covid-19 treatment. This emphasizes the need for extensive research on ASH-NCQDs for Covid-19 mitigation.

Akhila Nair: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Kanagaraj Kuppusamy: Writing - review & editing. Jozef T. Haponiuk: Formal analysis. Sabu Thomas: Validation. Lalitha Gnanasekaran: Data curation, Investigation. Manickam Selvaraj: Formal analysis. Sreeraj Gopi: Supervision.
